insights.insight: The protocol settles verified purchase confirmation at roughly $0.10 per receipt versus $5–$150 CPA for probabilistic ad tech attribution — a 50–200x cost delta that exists because the receipt is the conversion event, not an inference about it.

With Chrome joining Safari and Firefox in killing cross-site cookies, the ad industry's primary targeting primitive is gone — and device fingerprinting is degrading under privacy regulation, leaving verified purchase signals as the most durable first-party-equivalent data layer available.

The dataset is building a rural spine across 17 CBSAs with no dominant metro cluster — a geographic distribution that is structurally different from cookie- and device-based ad tech data, which skews toward dense urban markets where ad spend concentrates.

Fetch and Ibotta hold real, identity-attached purchase data but it is not queryable by third parties — the verified receipt dataset here is identity-free and cross-ecosystem, which is the attribute those platforms cannot offer brands without regulatory exposure.

Physical retail receipts (grocery, home improvement, club) and SaaS/dev-tool receipts exist in the same pipeline but have not yet merged into a single queryable layer; five open SaaS vendor requests sit unactivated, and their activation would produce the first verified cross-domain spend signal at scale.

One active predicate — a brand querying verified purchase history against the dataset — is the first confirmed commercial demand signal, indicating that at least one brand has moved from awareness to active data pull against 4,038 cryptographically verified receipts.
